content.text: The admissions process is the first point of contact between prospective families and the school community. It sets the tone for the partnership between home and school, establishing trust, transparency, and shared commitment to student success. A well-designed admissions policy ensures that:

items: Every child who can benefit from our Cambridge international education has equitable access to enrollment
Families understand our educational philosophy, expectations, and support systems
The school maintains appropriate class sizes and teacher-student ratios
Students are placed in environments where they can thrive academically, socially, and emotionally
The school complies with Indonesian regulations while upholding international accreditation standards
content.title: Purpose of Admissions Policy

content.text: Our admissions practices directly support the school's mission to develop globally competent, ethically grounded learners. The process is designed to identify students who will:

items: Benefit from the Cambridge curriculum combined with Indonesian national subjects
Contribute positively to our inclusive, multicultural learning community
Develop the six Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs): Academic Excellence, Communication, Global Competence, Ethical Leadership, Innovation & Creativity, and Wellbeing
Embody the values of Profil Pelajar Pancasila while developing international mindedness
content.title: Alignment with School Mission and SLOs

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to admitting students from diverse backgrounds, abilities, and experiences. Our admissions policy:

items: Does NOT discriminate based on religion, ethnicity, gender, socioeconomic status, or learning differences
DOES consider each applicant holistically, recognizing that academic potential manifests in different ways
Provides reasonable accommodations for students with Special Educational Needs (SEN)
Supports English as an Additional Language (EAL) learners through targeted placement and scaffolded instruction
Offers scholarship and financial aid opportunities where available, ensuring economic diversity
content.title: Commitment to Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

content.text: While maintaining rigorous academic standards consistent with Cambridge International education, we recognize that:

items: Entry assessments are diagnostic tools, not gatekeeping mechanisms
Students develop at different rates and come from varied educational backgrounds
EAL proficiency should not be confused with academic capability
With appropriate support, most students can succeed in an international curriculum
Our Multi-Tiered System of Support (MTSS) enables differentiated instruction for diverse learners (see Section 8)
content.title: Balance Between Academic Standards and Access

content.text: Open Enrollment Philosophy Eduversal Partner Schools operate on a philosophy of inclusive access, balanced with maintaining quality education:

items: We welcome students from diverse educational backgrounds, including Indonesian national schools, international schools, homeschool programs, and overseas institutions
We do not require entrance examinations for admission but use placement assessments for appropriate level assignment
We accept mid-year transfers when space permits and with appropriate transition support

content.text: However, schools must be transparent about:

items: Current capacity and realistic wait times
The academic demands of the Cambridge curriculum
Support services available and their limitations
Language proficiency expectations and support provided

content.text: Age Requirements by Year Level Schools must comply with both Cambridge International and the Indonesian Ministry of Education age requirements: Early Years Foundation Stage (EYFS):

items: Nursery: 3-4 years old (by September 1 of enrollment year)
Reception: 4-5 years old (by September 1 of enrollment year)

content.text: Primary (Cambridge Primary):

items: Year 1: 5-6 years old
Year 2: 6-7 years old
Year 3: 7-8 years old
Year 4: 8-9 years old
Year 5: 9-10 years old
Year 6: 10-11 years old

content.text: Lower Secondary (Cambridge Lower Secondary):

items: Year 7: 11-12 years old
Year 8: 12-13 years old

content.text: IGCSE (Upper Secondary):

items: Year 9: 13-14 years old
Year 10: 14-15 years old

content.text: AS & A Level:

items: Year 11 (AS): 15-16 years old
Year 12 (A2): 16-17 years old

content.text: Indonesian Compliance Note: Age verification must be documented with Akta Kelahiran (birth certificate). Age requirements align with Permendikbudristek regulations on PPDB (Penerimaan Peserta Didik Baru). Academic Readiness Criteria Rather than rigid cut-off scores, schools assess readiness holistically: For Primary Students:

items: Basic literacy and numeracy appropriate to previous grade level
Social-emotional readiness for classroom routines
Previous school reports (if available)
Parent input on learning preferences and challenges

content.text: For Secondary Students:

items: Review of previous school transcripts and academic records
Placement assessments in English and Mathematics
Subject-specific screening for science courses (Year 9 and above)
Interview with subject leaders or Principal

content.text: Important Principle: Past academic struggles should NOT automatically disqualify a student. Schools should consider:

items: Reasons for previous difficulties (e.g., language barriers, inadequate support, family circumstances)
Evidence of growth mindset and willingness to work hard
Availability of appropriate interventions at the school

content.text: EAL Proficiency Considerations English proficiency is assessed but NOT used as a barrier to admission:

items: Students are placed based on CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference) levels:
Primary: A1 (beginner) to B1 (intermediate)
Lower Secondary: A2 (elementary) to B2 (upper intermediate)
IGCSE: B1 (intermediate) to C1 (advanced)
A Level: B2 (upper intermediate) to C1 (advanced)
Schools provide tiered EAL support (see Section 8.4):
Tier 1: In-class differentiation and scaffolding
Tier 2: Small group pull-out sessions (3-5 students)
Tier 3: Intensive 1-on-1 language support
EAL students may need extended time (1-3 years) to reach grade-level academic language proficiency
Families are informed about realistic timelines for English fluency
Schools should NOT promise immediate fluency or refuse admission solely due to limited English

content.text: Assessment Note: Use language-reduced tasks when assessing EAL learners' content knowledge in mathematics or science. Do not conflate language proficiency with cognitive ability. SEN and Learning Support Access Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to inclusive education within our capacity: Admission Considerations for SEN Students:

items: Students with documented learning differences (e.g., dyslexia, ADHD, ASD) are welcome
Admission decisions are based on whether the school can reasonably meet the child's needs
Parents must provide complete documentation (psychological assessments, IEPs from previous schools)
A meeting between parents, Principal, and Learning Support Coordinator is required before final enrollment

content.text: Reasonable Accommodations Include:

items: Extended time on assessments
Modified assignments and differentiated materials
Assistive technology (text-to-speech, graphic organizers)
Preferential seating and movement breaks
Access to counseling and social-emotional support

content.text: Schools CANNOT Guarantee:

items: 1-on-1 teaching assistants (shadow teachers) unless externally funded by family
Curriculum modifications that compromise Cambridge assessment objectives
Specialized therapeutic services (e.g., speech therapy, occupational therapy) beyond basic school counseling

content.text: Honesty is Essential: If the school lacks capacity to support a student's needs, it is ethically responsible to recommend alternative placements rather than admit and provide inadequate support. For Cambridge Access Arrangements (exam accommodations), see Section 10.3.
Schools should request:

items: Official transcripts or report cards from previous school (last 2 years)
Conduct/behavior records
Teacher reference letters (optional but helpful)
Any documentation of SEN, 504 plans, or IEPs
content.title: Previous School Reports and References

content.text: Red Flags Requiring Further Investigation:

items: Unexplained gaps in school attendance
Multiple school transfers in short periods
Disciplinary issues related to bullying, violence, or academic dishonesty
Withdrawn from previous school under unclear circumstances

content.text: In such cases:

items: Contact previous school for clarification (with parent permission)
Conduct additional interviews with family and student
Require probationary enrollment period (e.g., one semester) with behavior contract
Be transparent with parents about concerns and expectations

content.text: Student Interview (for Year 4 and above):

items: Conducted by Principal, Subject Leader, or Homeroom Teacher
Assesses communication skills, interests, motivation, and fit
Informal and conversational, NOT interrogation
Sample questions:
"What subjects do you enjoy most and why?"
"Tell me about a challenge you've faced in school and how you handled it."
"What are you hoping to learn at this school?"
"What activities or hobbies do you enjoy outside school?"
content.title: Interview Process

content.text: Parent Interview:

items: Establishes shared understanding of school philosophy
Clarifies family expectations and school's offerings
Discusses student's learning profile, strengths, and needs
Reviews tuition, policies, and family responsibilities
Sample topics:
Family's educational values and aspirations for their child
Previous school experiences (positive and negative)
Home language and literacy practices
Any concerns about child's learning, behavior, or wellbeing
Understanding of Cambridge curriculum and boarding school life (if applicable)

content.text: Non-Discrimination Policy Eduversal Partner Schools explicitly prohibit discrimination in admissions based on:

items: Religion (schools welcome Muslim, Christian, Hindu, Buddhist, and other faiths)
Ethnicity or national origin
Gender or gender identity
Disability or learning differences
Socioeconomic status or ability to pay full tuition

content.text: Islamic Boarding Schools: While these schools incorporate Islamic values and practices, they may enroll non-Muslim students who respect and participate in the school community's Islamic ethos. Compliance: This policy aligns with Indonesian Constitution Article 31 (right to education) and Law No. 20/2003 on the National Education System. Sibling Priority and Legacy Considerations Many schools offer enrollment priority to:

items: Siblings of currently enrolled students
Children of alumni
Children of school staff

content.text: Benefits of Sibling Priority:

items: Simplifies family logistics (shared schedules, carpooling)
Strengthens school community cohesion
Provides continuity for families already invested in school culture

content.text: However:

items: Sibling priority does NOT mean automatic admission without assessment
If a sibling has significantly different learning needs beyond school capacity, admission may not be appropriate
Schools should be honest with families rather than admit students they cannot support

content.text: Tuition Transparency:

items: Full tuition and fee schedule must be published on school website and admissions materials
Breakdown of costs: tuition, enrollment fee, uniform, textbooks, trips, boarding (if applicable)
Payment plans and accepted payment methods
content.title: Financial Considerations and Scholarship Availability

content.text: Scholarships and Financial Aid:

items: Schools may offer merit-based or need-based scholarships (if Yayasan permits)
Scholarship criteria must be transparent and applied consistently
Financial need verification process (e.g., parent income documentation)
Scholarship recipients must maintain satisfactory academic progress and behavior

content.text: Important: Do NOT promise financial aid during admissions if funding is uncertain. Set realistic expectations about available support.
Placement assessments serve a diagnostic, NOT selective, function:

items: Determine appropriate year level for student (especially mid-year or transfer students)
Identify EAL support needs and CEFR level
Highlight strengths and areas needing additional support
Inform Tier 2 or Tier 3 intervention planning (see Section 8.2)
Establish baseline data for progress monitoring
content.title: Purpose of Placement Tests

content.text: Schools should communicate clearly to families: "These assessments help us understand your child's current learning profile so we can provide the right support. They are NOT tests to pass or fail."
Components of EAL Assessment: 1. Reading Comprehension:

items: Age-appropriate passages with comprehension questions
Assess literal understanding, inference, vocabulary
content.title: English Language Assessment

content.text: 2. Writing:

items: Prompt-based writing sample (e.g., "Describe your favorite holiday" for primary; "Explain a book you've read" for secondary)
Evaluate sentence structure, grammar, spelling, organization

content.text: 3. Speaking:

items: Conversational interview (part of student interview)
Assess fluency, pronunciation, vocabulary range

content.text: 4. Listening:

items: Short audio passage with follow-up questions
Evaluate comprehension of spoken English

content.text: CEFR Placement Rubric:

items: A1 (Beginner): Can understand and use familiar everyday expressions
A2 (Elementary): Can communicate in simple and routine tasks
B1 (Intermediate): Can deal with most situations while traveling; can produce simple connected text
B2 (Upper Intermediate): Can interact with fluency; can produce clear, detailed text on various subjects
C1 (Advanced): Can express ideas fluently and spontaneously; can produce well-structured, detailed text

content.text: Placement Recommendations by CEFR Level:

items: Primary (Years 1-6): A1-B1 acceptable with Tier 1-2 EAL support
Lower Secondary (Years 7-8): A2-B2; students below A2 need intensive Tier 3 support
IGCSE (Years 9-10): B1 minimum; B2 preferred for academic language demands
A Level (Years 11-12): B2 minimum; C1 preferred for essay writing and analysis
Assess numerical fluency, problem-solving, and conceptual understanding
Identify gaps from previous curriculum (especially for students from Indonesian national schools transitioning to Cambridge)
content.title: Mathematics Assessment

content.text: Primary (Years 1-6):

items: Number sense and operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division)
Fractions and decimals
Measurement and geometry basics
Simple word problems
content.title: Assessment Content by Level:

content.text: Lower Secondary (Years 7-8):

items: Algebraic expressions and equations
Ratio, proportion, percentages
Geometry (angles, area, volume)
Data interpretation (graphs, charts)

content.text: IGCSE (Years 9-10):

items: Algebra (linear equations, quadratic equations, simultaneous equations)
Trigonometry and coordinate geometry
Statistics and probability

content.text: A Level (Years 11-12):

items: Advanced algebra (functions, logarithms, exponential)
Calculus (differentiation, integration)
Mechanics and statistics (depending on pathway)

content.text: Language Consideration: Provide mathematics assessments in both English and Bahasa Indonesia for students with limited English proficiency. This ensures you are testing math ability, not just language comprehension. Other Subject Screening (for Mid-Year or Transfer Students) For students entering Year 9 (IGCSE) or Year 11 (A Level) mid-curriculum:

items: Assess prior knowledge in chosen subjects (e.g., Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Business, Geography)
Review previous course content against Cambridge syllabus requirements
Identify specific topics needing catch-up instruction

content.text: Example: A student transferring in Year 10 from Indonesian national curriculum may have strong biology knowledge but lack exposure to Cambridge-specific practical skills and exam techniques. Plan targeted bridging support. Accommodations for SEN Students During Testing To ensure fair assessment of SEN students' abilities:

items: Extended time (e.g., 25% or 50% additional time)
Quiet, distraction-free testing environment
Use of assistive technology (e.g., text-to-speech software, calculator)
Breaks during longer assessments
Scribe for students with writing difficulties
Enlarged print or colored overlays for students with visual processing needs

content.text: Document all accommodations provided during placement testing to inform future IEP development (see Section 8.3). Results Interpretation and Communication to Parents Placement Test Report Should Include:

items: Overall performance summary (strengths and areas for growth)
CEFR level for English (if applicable)
Recommended year level placement
Suggested support services (EAL, learning support, subject tutoring)
Baseline data for future progress monitoring

content.text: Sample Parent Communication: Based on [Student Name]'s placement assessments, we recommend enrollment in Year 7 with Tier 2 EAL support. [He/She] demonstrates strong mathematical reasoning and problem-solving skills. In English, [he/she] is currently at CEFR A2 level (elementary), which means [he/she] can communicate in simple, routine tasks but will need scaffolding for academic language. We will provide small-group EAL sessions three times per week, alongside in-class differentiation. We expect [Student Name] to reach B1 level (intermediate) within 12-18 months with consistent effort and support. Appeals Process for Placement Decisions If parents disagree with placement recommendations: 1. Schedule a follow-up meeting with Principal and relevant Subject Leaders 2. Review assessment results in detail, with examples of student work 3. Discuss parent concerns and rationale for school's recommendation 4. Consider trial period in requested level with progress monitoring 5. If disagreement persists, offer external assessment by educational psychologist (at family's expense) Final Authority: Principal makes the final placement decision in consultation with Subject Leaders and General Manager, prioritizing student's best interest and realistic chance of success.
Initial Application Application Form Requirements: The application form should be available in both English and Bahasa Indonesia and include: Student Information:

items: Full legal name (as on birth certificate)
Date of birth and place of birth
Gender
Home address and contact information
Nationality and passport/KTP number
NISN (Nomor Induk Siswa Nasional) if previously enrolled in an Indonesian school

content.text: Family Information:

items: Parent/guardian names, occupations, contact details (phone, email, WhatsApp)
Emergency contact information (minimum two contacts)
Sibling information (names, ages, schools)
Home language(s) spoken

content.text: Educational Background:

items: Previous schools attended (with dates)
Current year level
Subjects studied
Academic strengths and challenges
Any learning support or accommodations received

content.text: Health and Wellbeing:

items: Medical conditions, allergies, dietary restrictions
Medications taken regularly
Psychological or behavioral support needs
Any history of significant illness or injury

content.text: Additional Information:

items: Student interests, hobbies, extracurricular activities
Reasons for choosing this school
How family learned about the school

content.text: Required Documents For Indonesian Students:

items: Akta Kelahiran (Birth Certificate) \- original and photocopy
Kartu Keluarga (Family Card) \- photocopy
NISN (if applicable) \- from previous school
Previous school reports/transcripts (last 2 years)
School leaving certificate or transfer letter from previous school
Recent passport-size photographs (4 copies)
Immunization records (for Primary students)
Photocopy of parent/guardian KTP (Indonesian ID card)

content.text: For International/Expatriate Students:

items: Passport \- photocopy of student and parent
KITAS/KITAP (residence permit) \- photocopy
Birth certificate \- translated to Bahasa Indonesia if needed
Previous school reports/transcripts
School leaving certificate
Immunization records
English translation of all non-English documents

content.text: For Boarding Students (Additional):

items: Health certificate from licensed physician
Consent for medical treatment
Behavioral/disciplinary records from previous school

content.text: Indonesian Compliance Note: Ensure all documents comply with Permendikbudristek requirements for student data in the Dapodik system (see Section 2.4). Application Fee

items: Application processing fee: typically IDR 500,000 \- 1,000,000 (non-refundable)
This fee covers administrative costs of reviewing application, conducting assessments, and processing documents
Fee may be waived for scholarship applicants or families demonstrating financial hardship
Payment methods: bank transfer, cash, or online payment gateway

content.text: Timeline and Deadlines Rolling Admissions:

items: Most Eduversal Partner Schools operate on rolling admissions (applications accepted year-round, subject to space availability)
Priority enrollment periods:
October-December: Enrollment for following August intake
March-May: Enrollment for mid-year (January) intake
Late applications accepted but may have limited class availability

content.text: Key Dates (example timeline):

items: Application submission: ongoing
Placement assessments: scheduled within 2 weeks of application
Admission decision: communicated within 1 week of assessment
Enrollment confirmation: within 2 weeks of acceptance letter
New student orientation: 1 week before term starts

content.text: Enrollment Confirmation Acceptance Letter The acceptance letter should clearly state:

items: Student's full name and year level placement
Enrollment start date
Tuition and fee breakdown
Payment schedule and due dates
Enrollment deposit amount and deadline
Conditions of enrollment (e.g., submission of final transcripts, completion of orientation)
Contact information for questions

content.text: Sample Language: Congratulations! We are delighted to offer [Student Name] a place in Year 8 at [School Name], commencing August 2026. This offer is contingent upon submission of final school reports from [Previous School] and attendance at our New Family Orientation on [Date]. Please confirm your acceptance by submitting the signed enrollment contract and deposit of IDR [Amount] by [Deadline]. Enrollment Contract The contract should be a legally binding document (reviewed by Yayasan legal counsel) covering: Parties:

items: School (represented by Principal or General Manager)
Parent/Guardian (legal signatories)

content.text: Terms and Conditions:

items: Academic year dates and holiday schedule
Tuition amount and payment schedule
Refund policy and withdrawal procedures
Parent responsibilities (attendance, homework support, behavior expectations)
School responsibilities (quality education, safeguarding, communication)
Code of conduct and disciplinary procedures
Data privacy and photo/video consent
Agreement to abide by school policies (handbook provided)

content.text: Signature and Date:

items: Parent/guardian signature(s)
Principal or GM signature
Date of signing

content.text: Tuition Fee Structure and Payment Schedule Transparent Communication: Provide a detailed fee schedule including: 1. Annual Tuition:

items: Payable in 1, 2, or 3 installments (with early payment discount if applicable)
Includes: instruction, textbooks, basic extracurriculars

content.text: 2. Enrollment Fee (one-time, non-refundable):

items: New student registration processing

content.text: 3. Development Fee (annual):

items: Facilities maintenance and upgrades

content.text: 4. Additional Costs:

items: Uniforms (estimated cost with supplier information)
School trips and excursions
Exam fees (Cambridge IGCSE and A Level registration)
Boarding fees (if applicable): accommodation, meals, supervision
Private music lessons or specialized tutoring (optional)

content.text: Payment Methods:

items: Bank transfer to school account
Cash payment at finance office
Installment plans (if offered)

content.text: Late Payment Policy:

items: Grace period (e.g., 10 days after due date)
Late fee (e.g., 2% per month)
Consequences for non-payment (e.g., withholding report cards, exam entry, or continued attendance)

content.text: Be Compassionate: Offer payment plans or temporary relief for families facing genuine financial hardship, while maintaining firm boundaries to ensure school's financial sustainability. Refund and Withdrawal Policies Withdrawal Before Term Starts:

items: Enrollment deposit: forfeited
Tuition: fully refunded (if paid in advance)

content.text: Withdrawal During Term:

items: Tuition refund calculated on pro-rata basis for unused portion of term (e.g., if withdrawal in October, refund for November-July)
Enrollment and development fees: non-refundable
Notice period: minimum 1 month written notice required; otherwise, one month's tuition charged in lieu of notice

content.text: Exceptional Circumstances:

items: Medical emergencies, family relocation, or other documented hardships may be considered for partial refund on case-by-case basis
Final authority: Principal and General Manager

content.text: Clear Communication: Include refund policy in enrollment contract and on school website to prevent disputes. Parent Agreement to School Policies As part of enrollment, parents must acknowledge receipt and agreement to:

items: Student-Parent Handbook (rules, expectations, consequences)
Code of Conduct for Students
Uniform Policy
Attendance and Punctuality Policy
Homework Policy
ICT Acceptable Use Policy
Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy (see Section 9)
Anti-Bullying Policy
Complaints and Grievance Procedure

content.text: Format: Signed acknowledgement form or checkbox in enrollment contract stating, "I have read, understood, and agree to abide by all school policies as outlined in the Student-Parent Handbook." Data Privacy Consent Forms Required Consents (separate forms or checklist in enrollment packet): 1. General Data Processing Consent:

items: Storage and use of student personal data for educational purposes
Sharing data with Indonesian government (Dapodik), Cambridge International, and accreditation bodies
See Section 14 for full data protection policy

content.text: 2. Photo and Video Consent:

items: Use of student images in school website, social media, marketing materials
Use in classroom displays and school publications
Parents may opt out of external use while allowing internal use

content.text: 3. Medical Treatment Consent:

items: Emergency medical treatment authorization
Administration of basic medications (e.g., fever reducer, pain relief)
Communication with healthcare providers

content.text: 4. Trip and Activity Consent:

items: Participation in school trips and off-campus activities
Transportation arrangements
Supervision protocols

content.text: Parents must have the right to revoke consent at any time by written notice to the school. Medical and Emergency Contact Information Medical Information Form:

items: Student blood type
Allergies (food, medication, environmental)
Chronic health conditions (asthma, diabetes, epilepsy, etc.)
Current medications and dosage
Name and contact of family doctor
Health insurance information (if applicable)

content.text: Emergency Contacts:

items: Minimum 2 contacts (in addition to parents)
Names, relationship to student, phone numbers, addresses
Contacts must be reachable and able to collect student in emergency

content.text: Update Requirements: Parents must update this information immediately if changes occur during the school year.

New Family Orientation Sessions Schools should conduct bilingual (English and Bahasa Indonesia) orientation covering: For Parents: • Welcome from Principal and key staff introductions • Overview of Cambridge curriculum and Eduversal pedagogy • Academic expectations and homework guidelines • Communication channels (EduNav parent portal, email, WhatsApp groups) • Safeguarding and wellbeing support systems • Co-curricular programs and extracurricular opportunities • Logistics: drop-off/pick-up, uniforms, lunch, transportation For Students: • Meet the teacher/homeroom teacher • School tour (classrooms, library, science labs, sports facilities, clinic) • Introduction to school rules and routines • Peer buddy system (pair new students with current students) • Ice-breaker activities to build community For Boarding Students (Additional Session): • Tour of dormitories and facilities • Introduction to boarding staff and house parents • Daily routines (wake-up, meals, study time, lights out) • Boarding rules and expectations • Homesickness support strategies Timing: Conduct orientation 1 week before term starts or on first day for mid-year enrollments.
content.title: Student Onboarding

content.text: Ensure new families see:

items: Classrooms (primary, secondary, science labs)
Library and media center
Computer labs
Sports facilities (field, gymnasium, swimming pool if available)
Cafeteria or canteen
School clinic
Administration offices
Prayer rooms (especially in Islamic schools)
content.title: School Tour and Facilities Introduction

content.text: Highlight Safety Features:

items: Secure entry/exit points
CCTV coverage
Fire exits and emergency assembly points

content.text: For Primary Students:

items: Introduce to homeroom teacher (who teaches most subjects)
Provide class list and schedule
Share classroom expectations and routines
content.title: Meet the Teacher and Homeroom Assignment

content.text: For Secondary Students:

items: Introduce to homeroom teacher (pastoral care role)
Provide subject teacher list and timetable
Explain block schedule and movement between classrooms

content.text: Uniform Policy:

items: Provide uniform supplier contact information and price list
Specify required items (shirts, pants/skirts, PE uniform, shoes, tie, hijab options for Islamic schools)
Explain grooming standards (hair, jewelry, makeup)
content.title: Uniform and Materials Requirements

content.text: School Supplies:

items: List of required materials (notebooks, pens, calculators, art supplies)
Textbook distribution process (purchased vs. borrowed)
Device requirements (laptop/tablet for secondary students)

content.text: EduNav is Eduversal's quality management platform providing: For Parents:

items: Access to student attendance records
View grades and assessment results
Teacher feedback and progress reports
Upcoming events and school calendar
Communication with teachers and administration
Payment and billing information
content.title: Introduction to EduNav (Parent Portal)

content.text: Orientation Training:

items: Demonstrate how to log in and navigate portal
Show key features (attendance dashboard, gradebook, messaging)
Provide user guide in English and Bahasa Indonesia
Offer tech support contact for troubleshooting

content.text: Safeguarding Briefing for Parents Essential topics (see Section 9 for details):

items: School's commitment to safeguarding all students
Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) and how to report concerns
Safeguarding in boarders (for boarding schools)
Online safety and digital citizenship
Anti-bullying procedures
Parent responsibilities in safeguarding (monitoring social media, attending workshops)

content.text: Message to Convey: "Safeguarding is a partnership between home and school. We all have a role in keeping children safe."
For EAL Learners:

items: Initial EAL level assessment (already completed in placement testing)
Assignment to EAL support group (Tier 1, 2, or 3)
Schedule of EAL pull-out sessions (if applicable)
Introduction to bilingual support staff (if available)
Resources for home language practice (encourage maintaining mother tongue literacy)
content.title: Integration Support for EAL and New International Students

content.text: For International Students:

items: Cultural orientation to Indonesian customs and norms
Bahasa Indonesia introductory phrases and basic greetings
Peer mentors from same cultural background (if possible)
Connection to international student community or diversity club

content.text: For Transfer Students Mid-Year:

items: Catch-up plan for missed curriculum content
Subject-specific tutoring if needed
Social integration support (join clubs, assigned buddy)

content.text: Space Availability Assessment Before accepting mid-year transfers:

items: Check current class sizes against maximum capacity (Cambridge recommendation: 20-24 students per class)
Consider teacher-student ratios and workload
Assess whether adding a student will compromise quality of education for existing students
Priority for transfer students: siblings of current students, children of new staff members

content.text: If classes are at capacity:

items: Offer placement on waitlist for next academic year
Provide referrals to other Eduversal Partner Schools with openings
Remain transparent: "We would love to welcome your child, but our Year 7 class is currently full. We can add your child to the waitlist and notify you if a space becomes available."

content.text: Curriculum Alignment Students transferring from different curricula face adjustment challenges: From the Indonesian National Curriculum to Cambridge:

items: Greater emphasis on critical thinking, inquiry, and problem-solving (vs. rote memorization)
Increased English language demands
Different assessment formats (essays, practical exams, coursework)
More student-centered pedagogy

content.text: From Other International Curricula (IB, American, Australian):

items: Variations in subject content and sequencing
Different grading systems (e.g., IB 1-7 scale vs. Cambridge A-E grades)
Adjustment to Cambridge exam-focused approach in IGCSE and A Level

content.text: Schools should:

items: Conduct curriculum mapping to identify gaps and overlaps
Provide bridging materials for key concepts missed
Set realistic expectations with families about adjustment period (typically 1-2 terms)

content.text: For students transferring from other Cambridge schools:

items: Accept Cambridge Checkpoint or IGCSE results directly
Continue coursework and exam preparation without disruption
content.title: Academic Record Evaluation and Credit Transfer

content.text: For students from other curricula:

items: Evaluate transcripts against Cambridge syllabus requirements
Determine equivalent year level (may not always match age)
Decide which subjects can be credited and which require re-taking

content.text: Example: A Year 10 student transferring from IB MYP with strong science background may enter IGCSE Science without issue, but may need to catch up on specific Cambridge Physics or Chemistry content. Credit Transfer Policy:

items: No automatic credit transfer for non-Cambridge qualifications
Each case evaluated individually by Principal and Subject Leaders
Final decision based on student's best interest and realistic chance of success in Cambridge exams

content.text: For transfer students with curriculum gaps: 1. Diagnostic Assessment:

items: Administer placement tests (as in Section 11.3)
Identify specific topics or skills needing reinforcement
content.title: Catch-Up Support Planning

content.text: 2. Individualized Catch-Up Plan: \- Developed by Subject Leaders and Homeroom Teacher \- Specify topics to cover, resources to use, timeline for completion \- May include: after-school tutoring, weekend catch-up sessions, online resources, peer tutoring 3. Progress Monitoring: \- Weekly check-ins during first term \- Adjust support based on student's progress \- Involve parents in home practice and reinforcement 4. Realistic Timeline: \- Full curricular alignment may take 6-12 months \- Some content gaps may persist; focus on critical foundations Important: Do not overwhelm transfer students with excessive catch-up work. Prioritize essential content and allow time for social-emotional adjustment.

Transferring schools mid-year is socially and emotionally challenging: Social Integration Strategies: • Assign a peer buddy for the first 2-4 weeks • Introduce student to classmates and facilitate inclusion • Encourage participation in extracurricular activities and clubs • Monitor for signs of loneliness, anxiety, or social exclusion Homeroom Teacher Role: • Check in daily during first week, then weekly • Build rapport and trust • Communicate regularly with parents about adjustment • Refer to school counselor if needed (see Section 9 for wellbeing support) Parents' Role: • Maintain open communication with school • Reinforce positive messages about new school • Arrange playdates or social connections outside school • Be patient with adjustment period (typically 4-8 weeks)
content.title: Transition Support for Transfer Students

content.text: With Parent Permission: Contact previous school to request:

items: Official transcripts and academic records
Teacher recommendations and subject reports
Disciplinary records (if any)
IEP or learning support documentation
Any safeguarding concerns or incidents
content.title: Communication with Previous School

content.text: Confidentiality: Information from the previous school is used solely to support a student's transition and should be handled according to data protection guidelines (see Section 14). If Previous School is Non-Responsive:

items: Document attempts to contact (emails, phone calls)
Proceed with enrollment based on parent-provided information
Monitor student closely during probationary first term
Be alert for any discrepancies between parent account and student's actual performance/behavior

content.text: Honesty and Accuracy in All Marketing Materials: Eduversal Partner Schools must ensure that all promotional content—websites, brochures, social media posts, advertisements—accurately represent: 1. Facilities and Resources:

items: Show actual school facilities, not aspirational images from other schools
If using stock photos, clearly label as illustrative
Do not exaggerate (e.g., do not call a small library a "world-class media center")
content.title: Truth in Advertising

content.text: 2. Academic Programs:

items: Clearly state which Cambridge programs are offered (Primary, Lower Secondary, IGCSE, AS/A Level)
Specify which subjects are available (do not list subjects not currently taught)
Accurately represent teacher qualifications and experience

content.text: 3. Results and Achievements:

items: Report Cambridge exam results accurately (percentage of A-C grades, pass rates, average scores)
Do not cherry-pick only top performers
Provide context (e.g., "Out of 30 students, 25 achieved A-B grades in IGCSE Mathematics")
Celebrate achievements without misleading comparisons (e.g., do not claim "best in Indonesia" without verifiable data)

content.text: 4. University Placements:

items: List actual universities where alumni have been accepted
Do not imply guaranteed university admissions
Be honest about the range of destinations (local Indonesian universities, regional, international)

content.text: Permissible Statements: • "We are a Cambridge International School with Centre number ID123" • "Our students take Cambridge IGCSE and A Level examinations" • "We follow the Cambridge International curriculum" Prohibited Statements: • "We are a Cambridge University-affiliated school" (implies connection to University of Cambridge, which is false) • "Cambridge-certified school" (no such certification exists) • "Officially recognized by Cambridge University" (Cambridge Assessment is separate from the University) Compliance: Adhere to Cambridge International's branding and logo usage guidelines. Do not misuse Cambridge logo or trademarks.
content.title: No Misleading Claims About Cambridge Affiliation

content.text: For Newer Schools:

items: Be transparent about how long the school has been operating
Acknowledge which year groups are still growing (e.g., "We currently offer Years 1-8 and will expand to Year 9 in August 2026")
Do not claim track records that do not yet exist (e.g., do not advertise "proven success in A Levels" if no cohort has completed A Levels yet)
content.title: Honest Communication About School Stage of Development

content.text: For Established Schools:

items: Share historical data (e.g., "In our 10 years of operation, 95% of our students have been accepted to universities worldwide")
Highlight improvements over time (e.g., "Our IGCSE A-C pass rate has increased from 70% in 2020 to 85% in 2025")

content.text: Aspirations vs. Reality:

items: It is acceptable to share school vision and future plans (e.g., "We aim to open a STEAM Innovation Lab in 2027")
Clearly distinguish between current reality and future goals (e.g., "While we are working toward CIS accreditation, we are not yet accredited")

content.text: Schools must comply with: • Law No. 8/1999 on Consumer Protection: Prohibits false or misleading advertising • Indonesian Broadcasting Commission (KPI) guidelines (if advertising on TV/radio) • Ministry of Education regulations on educational institution advertising Key Principles: • Do not make claims that cannot be substantiated • Provide clear, comprehensible information (avoid jargon) • Do not exploit children in advertising (e.g., no manipulative emotional appeals)
content.title: Compliance with Indonesian Advertising Regulations

content.text: Written Parental Consent for Photos and Videos Before using any student images:

items: Obtain signed consent from parents (included in enrollment packet, see Section 11.4)
Specify intended uses:
School website and social media
Printed brochures and promotional materials
Internal school publications (yearbook, newsletter)
Media interviews or press coverage
content.title: Use of Student Images and Work

content.text: Consent Form Should Clarify:

items: Parents may opt out of external marketing use while allowing internal classroom use
Consent may be revoked at any time
Images will not be sold to third parties
Student names will not be published alongside photos unless separately consented

content.text: Safeguarding Consideration: NEVER publish student full names alongside photos in publicly accessible materials (e.g., on website). Use first names only or no names at all to protect student privacy. Student Privacy Protection Best Practices:

items: Avoid full facial close-ups; use group photos when possible
Do not publish sensitive personal information (home address, phone number, detailed family background)
If showcasing student work, remove any identifiable information (unless consent provided)
Store all media files securely with access controls (see Section 14)

content.text: Age-Appropriate Representation Ensure diversity and inclusivity in marketing materials:

items: Represent students of various ages, genders, ethnicities, and abilities
Avoid tokenism (e.g., do not feature only one student with disability to appear inclusive)
Portray students in authentic learning contexts (not overly posed or staged)
Avoid stereotypes (e.g., only boys in science labs, only girls in art classes)

content.text: Cultural Sensitivity For Islamic Schools:

items: Respect Islamic dress codes in photos (e.g., ensure hijab is worn by female students if required)
Showcase Islamic values and practices (prayer times, Quranic studies, Ramadan observance)
Avoid images that conflict with Islamic principles (e.g., mixed-gender physical contact in PE classes if not permitted)

content.text: For All Schools:

items: Be mindful of cultural norms around modesty and propriety
Celebrate diversity while respecting religious and cultural practices
Avoid religious or political messages that may alienate families

content.text: Website and Social Media Current and Accurate Information School website must include:

items: Up-to-date academic calendar
Current tuition and fee schedule
Staff directory with photos and qualifications
Curriculum overview and subject offerings
Admissions process and application forms (downloadable)
Contact information (phone, email, address, map)
Policies and handbooks (Student-Parent Handbook, Admissions Policy)

content.text: Update Frequency:

items: Critical information (calendar, fees): reviewed quarterly
News and events: updated weekly or as events occur
Staff directory: updated immediately when staff changes occur

content.text: Regular Updates Social Media Best Practices:

items: Post regularly (2-3 times per week minimum) to maintain engagement
Share diverse content: student achievements, classroom activities, staff spotlights, event photos
Celebrate festivals and national holidays (Indonesian Independence Day, Eid, Christmas, etc.)
Encourage respectful interaction; moderate comments to prevent bullying or inappropriate content

content.text: Content Calendar:

items: Plan posts in advance around key school events (admissions open days, exam results, sports day, cultural festivals)
Balance promotional content with authentic stories of student learning and growth

content.text: Bilingual Content (English and Bahasa Indonesia) Accessibility Requirement:

items: All critical information (admissions, tuition, policies) must be available in both languages
Use professional translation, not Google Translate
Ensure consistency between English and Bahasa Indonesia versions

content.text: Social Media Posts:

items: May alternate languages (some posts in English, others in Bahasa Indonesia)
For important announcements, post in both languages or provide translation in comments

content.text: Rationale: Eduversal Partner Schools serve diverse families, including those more comfortable in Bahasa Indonesia and those preferring English. Accessibility Features Website Accessibility (WCAG Compliance):

items: Text size adjustable
High contrast mode for visually impaired users
Alt text for all images (describe images for screen readers)
Keyboard navigation (for users unable to use a mouse)
Captions or transcripts for video content

content.text: Mobile Optimization:

items: Ensure website is mobile-friendly (many parents access information via smartphones)
Test website on multiple devices and browsers

content.text: Website Privacy Policy: • Clearly state how user data is collected, stored, and used • Provide an option to opt out of cookies or tracking • Link to full Data Protection Policy (see Section 14) Secure Forms: • Use SSL encryption for online application or inquiry forms • Do not request sensitive information (e.g., bank account details) via insecure channels Compliance: Align with Indonesian data protection norms and GDPR principles (even though Indonesia does not have GDPR, it is best practice for international schools). Prospective Parent Communications Transparent Fee Structure When Discussing Tuition with Prospective Parents:

items: Provide full breakdown of costs (tuition, enrollment fee, development fee, additional expenses)
Be upfront about potential additional costs (uniforms, trips, exam fees)
Clarify what is included in tuition (meals, textbooks, extracurriculars)
Explain payment schedules and installment options
content.title: Data Protection Compliance

content.text: Avoid Hidden Fees: Do not surprise families with unexpected charges after enrollment. Clear Admissions Criteria In Conversations with Prospective Families:

items: Explain placement assessment process and purpose
Be realistic about academic expectations (e.g., "Cambridge curriculum is rigorous; students should be prepared to work hard and manage challenging content")
Clarify EAL and SEN support availability and limitations
Share typical student profile (e.g., "Most of our students come from Indonesian national schools and are EAL learners; we provide strong language support")

content.text: Do Not Overpromise: If the school cannot guarantee a spot (e.g., due to space constraints), say so upfront.
Support Services Available:

items: EAL pull-out sessions and in-class scaffolding
Learning support for students with mild-to-moderate SEN
Counseling and pastoral care
Academic tutoring (if offered)
content.title: Honest Discussion of Student Support Services

content.text: Limitations to Acknowledge:

items: We do not provide specialized therapy (speech, occupational, behavioral)
We cannot support students with severe developmental disabilities requiring 1-on-1 aides
Our EAL support helps students develop English proficiency, but fluency takes 1-3 years
Learning support is group-based, not individualized (unless IEP requires it)

content.text: Transparency Builds Trust: Families appreciate honesty and are more likely to enroll if expectations are clear from the start.

Unethical Practices to Avoid: • "Only 2 spots left! Enroll today or lose your chance!" (unless genuinely true) • "Special discount only if you sign up this week" (arbitrary deadlines to pressure families) • "Other schools are inferior; we are the only good choice" (denigrating competitors) • Overly aggressive follow-up calls or messages after initial inquiry Ethical Enrollment Practices: • Provide accurate information about space availability • Allow families time to make informed decisions • Respect parents' choice to visit multiple schools before deciding • Follow up politely and professionally without badgering
content.title: No Pressure Tactics or False Urgency

content.text: After Initial Inquiry: • Send welcome email with key information (admissions process, tuition, school calendar) • Invite family to schedule school tour or open house • Provide contact for follow-up questions (Admissions Coordinator, Principal) After School Visit: • Send thank-you email • Offer to answer additional questions • Provide application form and checklist of required documents • Set clear timeline for next steps If Family Has Not Responded: • Send one polite follow-up reminder (1-2 weeks after initial contact) • Respect silence as potential disinterest; do not harass • Keep family on general mailing list for newsletters and event invitations (with opt-out option) Record-Keeping: • Maintain log of all communications with prospective families in CRM system or spreadsheet • Track inquiries, visits, applications, and enrollment decisions • Use data to evaluate effectiveness of marketing strategies
content.title: Professional Follow-Up Protocols

content.text: When to Use Waitlists: • Classes are at full capacity but there is potential for openings (due to withdrawals, transfers) • School wants to maintain interest from qualified applicants for future enrollment • Mid-year or late applications for high-demand year levels (e.g., Year 7, Year 9) Waitlist Process: 1. Inform Applicants:

items: Notify families that student is placed on waitlist (not rejected)
Explain: "We are unable to offer a spot at this time due to capacity constraints, but we would like to keep your application active in case a space becomes available."
content.title: Waitlist Management and Communication

content.text: 2. Waitlist Priority Criteria:

items: Date of application (first-come, first-served)
Sibling enrollment (priority for families already in school community)
Specific needs alignment (e.g., student with specific subject interests fitting available openings)

content.text: 3. Regular Updates:

items: Communicate with waitlisted families monthly (or quarterly)
Provide realistic expectations: "We typically see 2-3 openings per year in Year 8 due to family relocations."
Offer option to remain on waitlist for following academic year if no space materializes

content.text: 4. Notification of Opening:

items: Contact family immediately when space becomes available
Provide 3-5 business days to accept offer
If family declines, move to next person on waitlist

content.text: Transparency: Families appreciate clarity about their position on waitlist and realistic likelihood of admission.
In order of priority: 1. Siblings of Currently Enrolled Students:

items: Family already invested in school community
Simplifies logistics for family
content.title: Priority Criteria for Waitlist Movement

content.text: 2. Students with Specific Skill or Background Needed:

items: Example: Waitlist for Year 11 A Level; opening in Physics class; prioritize student with strong science background

content.text: 3. Date of Application:

items: First-come, first-served among similarly qualified applicants

content.text: 4. Hardship Cases:

items: Student urgently needs to transfer due to bullying, school closure, or family relocation

content.text: Important: Waitlist priority should NOT include:

items: Ability to pay full tuition (no "buying" a spot)
Family connections or donations (unless transparent and board-approved development policy exists)

content.text: When Schools May Reject Applications: Legitimate Reasons:

items: Student's needs exceed school's capacity to provide appropriate support (e.g., severe SEN requiring 1-on-1 aide)
Significant behavioral or disciplinary concerns from previous school suggesting student would be unsafe or disruptive
Lack of space in requested year level with no projected openings
content.title: Appeals Process for Rejected Applications

content.text: Illegitimate Reasons (Discrimination):

items: Student's religion, ethnicity, gender, or disability (see Section 11.2 for non-discrimination policy)
Family's socioeconomic status or inability to pay full tuition
Student's EAL status or limited English proficiency

content.text: Appeal Process: Step 1: Written Appeal Submission

items: Parents submit written appeal to Principal within 14 days of rejection notification
Appeal must state reasons for disagreement with decision
Provide any additional documentation or evidence not previously considered (e.g., updated medical reports, recommendation letters, proof of progress)

content.text: Step 2: Appeal Review Meeting

items: Principal and relevant staff (Subject Leaders, Learning Support Coordinator) review appeal
Schedule meeting with parents to discuss concerns
Consider additional assessment or trial period if appropriate

content.text: Step 3: Final Decision

items: Principal, in consultation with General Manager, makes final decision
Communicate decision in writing within 10 business days
If appeal is successful, proceed to enrollment
If appeal is denied, provide clear rationale and suggest alternative options (other schools, reapplication next year)

content.text: External Review Option:

items: If parents believe decision was discriminatory or unjust, they may appeal to Yayasan Board
Board conducts independent review and issues binding decision
Initial rejection notification: sent within 1 week of application review
Parent appeal submission: within 14 days of rejection
School appeal review and response: within 10 business days
Total appeals process: approximately 3-4 weeks
content.title: Timeline for Appeals Review

content.text: Expedited Appeals: In urgent cases (e.g., student needs immediate placement due to previous school closure), school may expedite review. Final Decision Authority Principal's Role: • Makes day-to-day admissions decisions for standard applications • Has authority to accept, reject, or waitlist applicants General Manager's Role: • Consulted for appeals, unusual cases, or policy exceptions • Ensures decisions align with Eduversal standards and Yayasan policies Yayasan Board's Role: • Serves as final external review for disputed cases • Ensures school adheres to non-discrimination and ethical principles • Does not interfere with routine admissions decisions Principle: Admissions decisions should be made by educational professionals (Principal, teachers) based on student's best interest, not by external pressures.

Notice Period Requirements Parent Responsibilities: • Parents must provide written notice (email or letter) to Principal and Finance Office at least 1 month before intended withdrawal date • Notice period ensures school can manage class sizes and plan for replacement enrollment • Failure to provide adequate notice results in forfeiture of one month's tuition (in lieu of notice clause in enrollment contract) Example: • Parent submits withdrawal notice on October 15, stating student will leave on November 20 • School charges tuition through November (even though student leaves mid-month) • If parent submits notice on October 15 but wants student to leave immediately (October 15), school charges full November tuition as penalty Exceptions to Notice Requirement: • Emergencies (serious family illness, sudden relocation) • School-initiated withdrawal (disciplinary expulsion—rare and only after due process, see Section 5) Exit Interview with Parents Purpose of Exit Interview: • Understand reasons for withdrawal (feedback for school improvement) • Ensure smooth transition for student and family • Maintain positive relationship (families may return or refer others) • Address any outstanding issues or concerns Interview Conducted by: • Principal or Admissions Coordinator • In-person, phone, or video call (based on family preference) Key Questions to Ask: 1. Reason for Withdrawal: \- Family relocation \- Financial constraints \- Academic fit (curriculum too challenging or not challenging enough) \- Social or behavioral concerns (bullying, peer conflicts) \- Dissatisfaction with teaching, communication, or school policies \- Logistical challenges (transportation, schedule) 2. Overall School Experience: \- What did your family appreciate most about our school? \- What areas could we improve? \- How would you describe your child's academic progress and social experience? \- Did you feel supported by teachers and administration? 3. Future Plans: \- Where is your child enrolling next? \- Can we assist with records transfer or recommendations? 4. Would You Recommend Our School? \- Net Promoter Score (NPS): "On a scale of 0-10, how likely are you to recommend our school to another family?" Document Exit Interview: • Record key feedback in confidential summary • Share themes with Senior Leadership Team for continuous improvement • Track withdrawal reasons over time (if multiple families leave for same reason, investigate root causes) Student Records Transfer School's Responsibility: Upon formal withdrawal request, school must provide: 1. Official Transcript: \- All grades and academic records from time enrolled at school \- Signed and stamped by Principal \- Provided in sealed envelope (for integrity) 2. Transfer Letter or School Leaving Certificate: \- Confirms student was enrolled from [start date] to [end date] \- States year level completed \- Indicates student left in good standing (unless disciplinary issues) 3. Attendance Records: \- Summary of attendance and punctuality 4. Copy of Student Records: \- For parent's personal records \- Includes: report cards, standardized test scores (Cambridge Checkpoint, EASE), IEP/ILP documents (if applicable) 5. Recommendation Letter (Optional): \- If requested by parents for new school application \- Highlight student's strengths, contributions, and character \- Be honest but constructive; avoid overly negative comments Timeline: • Provide records within 5-10 business days of written request • Expedite if urgent (student starting new school immediately) Records Retention: • School retains copies of all student records for minimum 5 years after withdrawal (see Section 14 for data retention policy) Release of Records to New School: • Only with written parental authorization • Send directly to new school's admissions office (not to parents, to ensure integrity)

Final Report Card: • Provide most recent term report (or mid-term progress report if withdrawal occurs mid-term) • Include teacher comments and attendance summary Certificates to Provide: • Cambridge Checkpoint certificates (if student completed Year 6 or Year 9) • IGCSE or A Level certificates (if exams completed) • Participation certificates for extracurricular activities, sports, or competitions • Character or service awards earned during enrollment Ensure all certificates are signed, dated, and bear official school seal.
content.title: Final Report and Certificates

content.text: Refer to Enrollment Contract and Section 11.4 for detailed refund policy: Tuition Refund: • Calculated on pro-rata basis for unused portion of term • If student withdraws in October, refund for November-July tuition (if paid in advance) • Deduct one month's tuition if notice period not met Non-Refundable Fees: • Enrollment fee • Development fee • Exam fees (if already registered with Cambridge) Refund Processing Timeline: • Refunds processed within 30 days of withdrawal confirmation • Paid via bank transfer or check to parent's account Outstanding Balances: • Any unpaid tuition, fees, or fines must be settled before records are released • School may withhold transcripts and certificates until account is cleared
content.title: Refund Policy Application

content.text: Maintaining Alumni Relationships: • All students who complete at least one full year at the school are considered alumni • Invite alumni to join school alumni network (email list, WhatsApp group, LinkedIn group) • Encourage alumni to stay connected and return for school events Alumni Benefits: • Invitations to annual alumni gathering or reunion • Eligibility for alumni mentorship program (alumni mentor current students) • Priority enrollment for siblings or children of alumni • Networking opportunities with other alumni Alumni Database: • Maintain contact information (with consent) for alumni outreach • Track alumni university placements and career paths • Celebrate alumni achievements in school newsletters and social media Goodwill Message: "Even though your child is leaving, they will always be part of our school family. We wish them every success and hope they will stay in touch."
content.title: Alumni Status and Continued Connection

content.text: GDPR-Aligned Practices Even though Indonesia does not have GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), Eduversal Partner Schools adopt GDPR-inspired principles as best practice for international schools: Core Principles: 1. Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency: \- Data collection has legitimate educational purpose \- Parents are informed about what data is collected and why \- Privacy policies are written in clear, accessible language 2. Purpose Limitation: \- Student data used only for stated purposes (education, assessment, safety) \- Not sold or shared with third parties for marketing 3. Data Minimization: \- Collect only data necessary for educational functions \- Do not request excessive or irrelevant information 4. Accuracy: \- Maintain up-to-date student records \- Allow parents to review and correct inaccuracies 5. Storage Limitation: \- Retain student records only as long as legally required or educationally necessary \- Securely delete data after retention period (see records retention schedule below) 6. Integrity and Confidentiality: \- Protect student data with appropriate security measures \- Restrict access to authorized personnel only 7. Accountability: \- School is responsible for demonstrating compliance with data protection principles \- Regular audits and Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs)

Data Collected During Admissions: Personal Information: • Student name, date of birth, gender, nationality • Home address, contact information • Parent names, occupations, contact details • Previous schools attended • NISN (for Indonesian students) Sensitive Information: • Health records and medical conditions • Learning support needs and psychological assessments • Behavioral or disciplinary records • Financial information (for scholarship applications) Security Measures: Physical Security: • Admissions files stored in locked cabinets in secure office • Access restricted to Admissions Coordinator, Principal, and authorized administrative staff Digital Security: • Student data stored in password-protected database (EduNav or school management system) • Multi-factor authentication for admin users • Regular backups to secure server • Encrypted transmission of sensitive documents (e.g., via secure portal, not regular email) Best Practice: Do NOT send student medical records, psychological assessments, or financial documents via unencrypted email. Use secure file-sharing platforms (Google Drive with restricted access, password-protected attachments) or collect documents in person.
content.title: Student Data Security in the Admissions Process

content.text: Required Consents (obtained during enrollment, see Section 11.4): 1. General Data Processing:

items: Storage and use of student information for educational, administrative, and safety purposes

The consent form should specify: "We will collect and store your child's personal information (name, date of birth, academic records, health information) to provide quality education, ensure safety, and comply with legal requirements."
content.title: Parent Consent for Data Use

content.text: 2. Data Sharing with Third Parties:

items: Specify which external entities will receive student data:
Cambridge International (exam registration)
Indonesian Ministry of Education (Dapodik reporting)
Accreditation bodies (CIS, WASC) during site visits
Insurance companies (for student accident insurance)
Educational service providers (e.g., school bus company, field trip organizers)
Obtain separate consent for each category or provide opt-out options

content.text: 3. Photo and Video Use:

items: Separate consent for marketing and promotional use (see Section 11.6)

content.text: Parents' Rights:

items: Right to access their child's data (request copy of student file)
Right to correct inaccuracies (update contact information, medical records)
Right to restrict processing (opt out of non-essential data uses)
Right to withdraw consent (e.g., revoke photo consent at any time)
Right to data portability (receive student records in transferable format)

content.text: How Long to Keep Student Records: Active Students: • All records maintained in current student file • Updated annually (new report cards, health forms, etc.) Withdrawn or Graduated Students: Minimum Retention Periods (after departure): Secure Disposal After Retention Period:

items: Physical records: shredding
Digital records: secure deletion (not just "delete"; use data wiping software)
content.title: Data Retention Policies

content.text: Storage Location: Archived student records stored in climate-controlled, secure facility (off-site or locked storage room) with restricted access.
Entities with Legitimate Access to Student Data: 1. Cambridge Assessment International Education:

items: Purpose: Exam registration, results processing
Data shared: Student name, date of birth, exam entries, candidate numbers
Compliance: Cambridge's own data protection policies apply
content.title: Third-Party Data Sharing

content.text: 2. Indonesian Ministry of Education (Kemendikbudristek):

items: Purpose: Dapodik (national student database) reporting
Data shared: Student demographics, enrollment status, attendance, NISN
Compliance: Indonesian Law No. 20/2003 on National Education System

content.text: 3. Accreditation Bodies (CIS, WASC):

items: Purpose: School evaluation and accreditation site visits
Data shared: Student work samples, assessment data, demographics (anonymized when possible)
Compliance: CIS and WASC confidentiality agreements

content.text: 4. Insurance Companies:

items: Purpose: Student accident or health insurance coverage
Data shared: Student name, date of birth, school enrollment status
Compliance: Insurance provider's data protection policies

content.text: 5. Educational Service Providers:

items: Purpose: School trips, transportation, catering, online learning platforms
Data shared: Minimal information needed for service delivery
Compliance: Data processing agreements with vendors (stipulate confidentiality, security measures, no secondary use of data)

content.text: Important: Do NOT share student data with vendors for marketing purposes (e.g., do not give student email lists to tutoring companies, even if they offer commissions). Incident Response In the event of a data breach (unauthorized access, loss, or theft of student data): 1. Immediate Actions: \- Secure affected systems (shut down compromised server, change passwords) \- Assess scope of breach (how many records affected, what data exposed) \- Contain breach to prevent further damage 2. Notification: \- Notify affected families within 72 hours \- Inform Indonesian data protection authorities (if applicable) \- Report to Yayasan Board and Eduversal management 3. Remediation: \- Investigate root cause (hacking, human error, system vulnerability) \- Implement corrective measures (strengthen security, staff training) \- Offer support to affected families (credit monitoring if financial data compromised) 4. Documentation: \- Maintain incident log with timeline, actions taken, and lessons learned \- Use for continuous improvement of data security practices For more details on data protection policies and procedures, see Section 14.
Penerimaan Peserta Didik Baru (PPDB) Compliance Indonesian Ministry of Education Regulation on Student Admissions: Key Requirements (per Permendikbudristek on PPDB): 1. Non-Discrimination:

items: Schools may not refuse admission based on religion, ethnicity, gender, disability, or socioeconomic status
Private schools (including Eduversal Partner Schools) have more flexibility than public schools but must still uphold equity principles
content.title: Indonesian Requirements

content.text: 2. Age Requirements:

items: Entry to Grade 1 (Primary): minimum age 6 years (by July 1 of enrollment year)
Entry to Grade 7 (Secondary): maximum age 15 years
Entry to Grade 10 (High School): maximum age 18 years

content.text: 3. Transparent Admissions Process:

items: Criteria for admission must be publicly available (on school website, in brochures)
Selection process must be objective, accountable, and free from corruption

content.text: 4. Priority for Local Students:

items: Public schools prioritize students from local catchment area
Private schools not bound by zoning but may prioritize local community for accessibility

content.text: 5. Registration Timeline:

items: National PPDB period: typically May-June for August intake
Schools should align admissions cycle with national timeline (though private schools have more flexibility)

content.text: Compliance Actions for Eduversal Schools:

items: Publish admissions policy on website in Bahasa Indonesia
Ensure PPDB Committee (Panitia PPDB) is formed with clear roles (Principal, Admissions Coordinator, Subject Leaders)
Document admissions decisions with rationale (especially rejections)
Report new student enrollments to local Education Office (Dinas Pendidikan)

content.text: NISN (Nomor Induk Siswa Nasional) is the unique national student identification number: When to Register for NISN:

items: All students enrolled in Indonesian schools (public or private) must have NISN
NISN requested for new students within 30 days of enrollment
Students transferring from other Indonesian schools already have NISN (school requests transfer from previous school)
content.title: NISN Registration Process

content.text: Registration Process: 1. Collect Required Documents: \- Student Akta Kelahiran (birth certificate) \- Kartu Keluarga (family card) \- Completed NISN application form 2. Submit to Dapodik: \- School Dapodik operator enters student data into system \- System generates NISN automatically (if student is new to Indonesian education system) \- Verify NISN accuracy and link to student profile 3. NISN Verification: \- Check NISN status on official portal: https://nisn.data.kemdikbud.go.id/ \- Ensure NISN is active and correctly linked to school 4. Update NISN Annually: \- Each semester, verify all student NISN records in Dapodik are current \- Update if student changes name, year level, or status International Students:

items: Students who are not Indonesian citizens and do not intend to pursue Indonesian higher education may be exempt from NISN
However, if international students study in Indonesia long-term, NISN registration is recommended for continuity

content.text: For more details on Dapodik compliance, see Section 2.4. Dapodik Data Entry for New Students Dapodik (Data Pokok Pendidikan) is the Indonesian Ministry's national education database: Data Entry Requirements for New Admissions: Student Demographics: • Full name, NISN, date of birth, gender • Nationality, religion • Home address, parent/guardian contact information Enrollment Status: • School entry date, current year level • Homeroom teacher assignment • Boarding or day student status Academic Records: • Previous school name and NPSN (if applicable) • Grades and attendance (updated each semester) Timeline: • New student data must be entered within 30 days of enrollment • Dapodik updated three times per year: beginning of Semester 1 (August), beginning of Semester 2 (January), and end of academic year (June) Compliance Check: Principal and Dapodik operator should conduct quarterly audits to ensure all enrolled students are accurately recorded in Dapodik. BAN-S/M Admissions Standards BAN-S/M (Badan Akreditasi Nasional Sekolah/Madrasah) is Indonesia's national school accreditation body: BAN-S/M Standards Related to Admissions: Standard 1: Admissions and Capacity • School has clear, written admissions policy • Admissions process is transparent, accountable, and non-discriminatory • Class sizes are appropriate (maximum 28-32 students per class) • Teacher-student ratios meet national standards (1:20 for primary, 1:24 for secondary) Standard 2: Documentation • Student files are complete (birth certificate, previous school reports, health records) • Enrollment contracts are signed and stored securely • Admissions records are organized and accessible for audit Standard 3: Equity and Inclusion • School provides access to students with diverse backgrounds and abilities • Scholarship or financial aid opportunities are available (if feasible) • Students with SEN are supported appropriately Evidence for BAN-S/M Accreditation Visit: • Admissions Policy document (in Bahasa Indonesia) • Sample application forms and enrollment contracts • Records of admissions decisions (anonymized) • Statistics: number of applicants, acceptance rate, demographic diversity • Documentation of SEN support and accommodations provided Cambridge Requirements Centre Registration of Candidates Cambridge Exam Registration Requirements: Schools must register students (candidates) for Cambridge exams by published deadlines: Entry Deadlines (approximate): • March Series (if offered): October of previous year • June Series: February • November Series: August Registration Process: 1. Collect Candidate Information: \- Full legal name (as on passport or birth certificate) \- Date of birth \- Gender \- Candidate number (assigned by school; sequential numbering system) 2. Enter Data into Cambridge Portal: \- Exams Officer logs into Cambridge International Direct portal \- Enters candidate details and exam entries (subjects, components) \- Reviews for accuracy (name spelling, date of birth, subject codes) 3. Pay Exam Fees: \- Calculate total fees (Cambridge charges per subject per candidate) \- Submit payment to Cambridge by deadline \- Late entries incur additional fees 4. Verify Entries: \- Cambridge sends confirmation statement \- Cross-check against school records \- Report discrepancies immediately to Cambridge Admissions Implication: • Students enrolling in Year 10 (IGCSE) or Year 11 (A Level) mid-year may miss exam registration deadlines • Schools must inform families: "If your child enrolls after [date], they may not be able to sit exams in June and will need to wait until November series or following year."

Cambridge requires precise personal information for exam certificates: Critical Data: • Legal name (as it will appear on certificate) • Date of birth (used for age verification and special consideration requests) • Gender (for statistical reporting; use "other" if preferred) Common Errors to Avoid: • Misspelled names (verify against official documents) • Incorrect date of birth (can cause issues with university applications) • Inconsistent name formatting (e.g., "Muhammad Ali" vs. "Muhamad Ali") Best Practice: During admissions, collect photocopy of passport or birth certificate and use this as definitive source for Cambridge registration.
content.title: Accurate Candidate Information

content.text: Admissions Cycle Must Consider Cambridge Timelines: For New Year 10 Students (IGCSE):

items: Ideally enroll before February to ensure June exam registration (for subjects taken in Year 10, if applicable)
If enrolling after February, student will take full IGCSE exam load in Year 11 (June and November series)
content.title: Timeline Alignment with Exam Registration Deadlines

content.text: For New Year 11 Students (IGCSE or AS Level):

items: Must enroll by February to register for June exams
Late enrollments (after February) may result in students sitting exams in November or waiting until following year

content.text: For New Year 12 Students (A Level):

items: Enroll by February for June A2 exams
Extremely difficult to accommodate mid-year transfers in Year 12 (students would have missed AS coursework and assessments)

content.text: Admissions Communication: Clearly inform prospective families of these constraints during initial inquiry and application process.

Fair and Transparent Admissions CIS (Council of International Schools) and WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges) Accreditation Standards: Standard: Access and Equity • School has written admissions policy that is fair, transparent, and consistently applied • Admissions criteria are clearly communicated to prospective families • School does not discriminate based on protected characteristics (race, religion, gender, disability, nationality) Standard: Enrollment Management • School maintains appropriate enrollment levels to ensure quality education • Class sizes and teacher-student ratios support effective learning • Waitlist management is ethical and transparent Standard: Marketing Integrity • School's promotional materials are accurate and not misleading • Claims about academic results, facilities, and programs are substantiated • Use of student images and work complies with privacy and consent requirements Evidence for CIS/WASC Accreditation: • Admissions Policy (including non-discrimination statement) • Admissions process flowchart (from inquiry to enrollment) • Decision records (show consistency and fairness) • Marketing materials review log (approval process for brochures, website content) • Parent consent forms for photo/video use
content.title: CIS/WASC Standards

content.text: Required Policy Elements: Statement of Inclusion: "[School Name] welcomes students of all backgrounds, abilities, and identities. We do not discriminate on the basis of race, color, national or ethnic origin, religion, gender, gender identity or expression, sexual orientation, age, disability, or socioeconomic status in admissions or any school programs and activities."
